items.q: Why is understanding patronage important for studying historical art?
items.correct: Patrons commissioned art for specific purposes, shaping content and form.
items.misconception: Artists worked freely - patronage was irrelevant to the artwork.
items.why: Students romanticise the lone artist. Most pre-modern art was COMMISSIONED, with patrons specifying subject, materials, even composition. Understanding the commission illuminates choices the artist made.

items.q: What is the difference between relief and intaglio printing?
items.correct: Relief prints from raised surfaces; intaglio prints from incised lines below the surface.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Relief is for paper, intaglio is for fabric.
items.why: Students don't grasp the inverse logic. Lino cuts AWAY non-printing areas (relief - raised inked); etching incises lines that HOLD ink below the surface (intaglio - ink pressed up into paper).

items.q: Which colours typically advance in a composition?
items.why: Students conflate darkness with advancement. In atmospheric perspective, COOLER colours recede (think distant blue mountains) and warmer colours advance, regardless of darkness.
It's Blue. “Red” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Picasso's Blue Period was famous for what mood?
items.why: Correct answer: Sadness. “Happiness” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly which year did Picasso's Blue Period begin?
items.why: The real figure is 1901. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
